Initial broad research memo
Overview: In 2017, under CIA Director Mike Pompeo, the agency developed plans to kidnap Julian Assange from the Ecuadorian Embassy in London, with internal discussions of assassination never forwarded to the White House, driven by rage over WikiLeaks' Vault 7 leak exposing CIA hacking tools—the agency's largest data loss. These extreme plots, part of a broader CIA 'war' on WikiLeaks including surveillance and discord-sowing, faced White House pushback and British constraints, ultimately failing; the US instead expedited legal charges, leading to Assange's 2019 embassy expulsion, arrest, and 2024 plea deal.
Origin premise: Julian Assange founded WikiLeaks in 2006 as a secure platform for whistleblowers to anonymously publish classified documents, embodying a radical transparency ethos that challenged state secrecy and later threatened US intelligence by enabling massive leaks like Chelsea Manning's war logs (2010) and Vault 7 (2017).
Key facts:
- CIA under Pompeo planned Assange's abduction from Ecuadorian Embassy, with internal talk of assassination not sent to White House.
  source: Yahoo News via Democracy Now
  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high
- Vault 7 leak in 2017, published by WikiLeaks, exposed CIA hacking tools and was called 'largest data loss in CIA history,' enraging Pompeo.
  source: Yahoo News via Democracy Now
  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high
- CIA conducted surveillance inside Embassy and on Assange associates; plans included multi-pronged attack like stealing computers.
  source: Yahoo News via Democracy Now
  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high
- Fear of Russian extraction of Assange in 2017 ramped up plans, including potential London shootout coordinated with UK (who insisted on handling any shooting).
  source: Yahoo News via Democracy Now
  url: https://www.democracynow.org/2021/9/28/cia_julian_assange_assassination_plot
  confidence: high
- White House lawyers objected; Justice Dept fast-tracked charges to preempt illegal ops.
  source: RSF and Yahoo News
  url: https://rsf.org/en/alarming-reported-cia-plot-against-julian-assange-exposed
  confidence: high
- Assange in Embassy since 2012 to avoid Swedish extradition (dropped 2017); expelled 2019, pleaded guilty 2024.
  source: Wikipedia and multiple
  url: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julian_Assange
  confidence: high
Tensions and contradictions:
- CIA's vengeful 'war' on WikiLeaks clashed with legal/diplomatic norms, sparking Trump-era White House debates and Justice Dept pivot to prosecution.
- Pompeo's personal embarrassment over Vault 7 drove detached-from-reality plots, but UK allies refused US shootouts and Russian fears proved unfounded.
- Assange's transparency mission exposed US war crimes (2010 leaks) yet CIA framed him as Russian asset, inverting leaker into national security threat.
Broader system: US intelligence community's institutional intolerance for leaks exposing operational tools and war crimes, revealing a shadow war on transparency platforms where vengeance overrides law, expediting legal rails when rendition/assassination risks blowback amid allied constraints and domestic oversight.
Runway beats that make the headline legible:
- WikiLeaks rises via 2010 Manning leaks (Collateral Murder, war logs, cables), making Assange a foe but not yet CIA-personal until 2017 Vault 7 on Pompeo's watch.
- Assange seeks Ecuador asylum 2012 amid Swedish probe (dropped 2017), creating embassy pressure-cooker.
- Vault 7 leak ignites Pompeo's vendetta, launching CIA ops amid 2016 election Russia tensions.
Turning points that changed the incentives or coalition:
- Vault 7 publication (March 2017): Shifts Assange from State/DoD target to CIA's existential threat under new director Pompeo.
- Credible intel on Russian extraction plot (late 2017): Escalates White House involvement, debating gun battles and plane sabotage.
- Ecuador embassy expulsion (April 2019): Ends siege, enabling UK arrest and US extradition push.
Stake-shifting moves and countermeasures:
- Ecuadorian Embassy asylum (2012): Deters extradition, forces CIA rendition plots.
- UK insistence on no US shootouts (2017): Blocks violent ops, channels to legal route.
- Justice Dept charges (sealed 2017, unsealed 2019): Stake-shift from covert kill/kidnap to overt prosecution.
- Assange's 2024 guilty plea under Biden: Resolves via softer leverage after Trump-era failures.
Actual resolution mechanism: CIA assassination/kidnap plots abandoned amid legal/diplomatic pushback; Justice Dept expedited Espionage Act charges (2017), Ecuador expelled Assange (2019), UK arrested him, leading to years of battles ending in 2024 plea deal for time served—system neutralized threat via lawfare without dramatic violence.
Open questions:
- Did Russian extraction intel justify escalation, or was it pretext for Pompeo's vendetta?
- Extent of implemented CIA surveillance and its role in Assange's psychological toll/pre-2019 expulsion?
- Post-plea: Will US pursue WikiLeaks remnants or has transparency threat been structurally deterred?

- Vault 7: Pompeo's Blood Oath and CIA's Shadow War: Core trigger beat; exposes institutional drift from leak embarrassment to abduction plots, with contradictions in CIA-White House tensions.
- Embassy Siege: Pressure Cooker and Russian Ghost: Builds confinement setup, stake-shifters like UK limits and false Russian intel, explaining hesitation/escalation dynamics.
- Failed Fantasies: Assassination Debates and Legal Pivot: Operational climax; highlights power contradictions, incentives for quieter win via charges over shootout.
- Lawfare Victory: The Real System Closes In: Resolution mechanism; contrasts headline 'failure' with system's success, avoiding epilogue hijack while showing hidden consequences.

Broader system: US intelligence community's institutional intolerance for leaks exposing operational tools and war crimes, revealing a shadow war on transparency platforms where vengeance overrides law, expediting legal rails when rendition/assassination risks blowback amid allied constraints and domestic oversight.

- WikiLeaks rises via 2010 Manning leaks (Collateral Murder, war logs, cables), making Assange a foe but not yet CIA-personal until 2017 Vault 7 on Pompeo's watch.
- Assange seeks Ecuador asylum 2012 amid Swedish probe (dropped 2017), creating embassy pressure-cooker.
- Vault 7 leak ignites Pompeo's vendetta, launching CIA ops amid 2016 election Russia tensions.
Turning points that changed the incentives or coalition:
- Vault 7 publication (March 2017): Shifts Assange from State/DoD target to CIA's existential threat under new director Pompeo.
- Credible intel on Russian extraction plot (late 2017): Escalates White House involvement, debating gun battles and plane sabotage.
- Ecuador embassy expulsion (April 2019): Ends siege, enabling UK arrest and US extradition push.
Stake-shifting moves and countermeasures:
- Ecuadorian Embassy asylum (2012): Deters extradition, forces CIA rendition plots.
- UK insistence on no US shootouts (2017): Blocks violent ops, channels to legal route.
- Justice Dept charges (sealed 2017, unsealed 2019): Stake-shift from covert kill/kidnap to overt prosecution.
- Assange's 2024 guilty plea under Biden: Resolves via softer leverage after Trump-era failures.
Actual resolution mechanism: CIA assassination/kidnap plots abandoned amid legal/diplomatic pushback; Justice Dept expedited Espionage Act charges (2017), Ecuador expelled Assange (2019), UK arrested him, leading to years of battles ending in 2024 plea deal for time served—system neutralized threat via lawfare without dramatic violence.
